analogy: Think of "Nutrition in Plants" through the chapter's own opening images: Nutrition in Plants 1 I n Class VI you learnt that food is essential for all living organisms. You also learnt that carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins and minerals are components of food. These components of food are called nutrients and are necessary for our body.... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 1.1 M ODE OF NUTRITION IN PLANTS, 1.2 P HOTOSYNTHESIS — F OOD, M AKING P ROCESS IN PLANTS, SCIENCE 2. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Nutrition in Plants is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that nutrition in Plants 1 I n Class VI you learnt that food is essential for all living organisms.

The text explains that you also learnt that carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins and minerals are components of food.

The text explains that these components of food are called nutrients and are necessary for our body.

The text explains that plants can synthesise food for themselves but animals including humans cannot.

The text explains that they get it from plants or animals that eat plants.

The text explains that thus, humans and animals are directly or indirectly dependent on plants.

The text explains that 1.1 M ODE OF NUTRITION IN PLANTS Plants are the only organisms that can prepare food for themselves by using water, carbon dioxide and minerals.

The text explains that the raw materials are present in their surroundings.

The text explains that the nutrients enable living organisms to build their bodies, to grow, to repair damaged parts of their bodies and provide the energy to carry out life processes.

The text explains that nutrition is the mode of taking food by an organism and its utilisation by the body.

The text explains that the mode of nutrition in which organisms make food themselves from simple substances is called autotrophic (auto = self; trophos = nourishment) nutrition.

The text explains that animals and most other organisms take in food prepared by plants.

The text explains that they are called heterotrophs (heteros = other).

The text explains that boojho wants to know how plants prepare their own food.

The text explains that now we may ask where the food factories of plants are located: whether food is made in all parts of a plant or only in certain parts?

The text explains that how do plants obtain the raw materials from the surroundings?

The text explains that water and minerals present in the soil are absorbed by the roots and transported to the leaves.

The text explains that carbon dioxide from air is taken in Paheli wants to know why our body cannot make food from carbon dioxide, water and minerals like plants do.

The text explains that water and minerals are transported to the leaves by the vessels which run like pipes throughout the root, the stem, the branches and the leaves.

The text explains that you will learn more about transport of materials in plants in Chapter 7.

The text explains that so we find that chlorophyll, sunlight, carbon dioxide and water are necessary to carry out the process of photosynthesis.

The text explains that thus, sun is the ultimate source of energy for all living organisms.

The text explains that can you imagine life on earth in the absence of photosynthesis!

The text explains that the survival of almost all living organisms directly or indirectly depends upon the food made by the plants.

The text explains that most cells have a distinct, centrally located spherical structure called the nucleus (Fig.

The text explains that 1.1 Cell Boojho wants to know how water and minerals absorbed by roots reach the leaves.

The text explains that in the absence of photosynthesis, life would be impossible on the earth.

The text explains that the process can be represented in an equation: During the process oxygen is released.

The text explains that 1.3 Diagram showing photosynthesis Light energy Carbon dioxide Oxygen Chlorophyll in leaf Water and minerals Stoma Guard Cells Chlorophyll

The text explains that do you see a yellow wiry branched structure twining around the stem and branches of a tree?

The text explains that synthesis of plant food other than carbohydrates You have just learnt that plants synthesise carbohydrates through the process of photosynthesis.

The text explains that you should think about it and discuss with your teacher. hair which are directed downwards.

The text explains that the pitcher-like or jug-like structure is the modified part of leaf.

The text explains that you learnt that plants absorb minerals and nutrients from the soil.

The text explains that the fungus provides shelter, water and minerals to the alga and, in return, the alga prepares and provides food to the fungus.

The text explains that sCIENCE 8 What you have learnt  All organisms need food and utilise it to get energy for growth and maintenance of their body.  Green plants synthesise food for themselves by the process of photosynthesis.

The text explains that they take food from the host plant.  Plants use simple chemical substances like carbon dioxide, water and minerals for the synthesis of food.  Chlorophyll, water, carbon dioxide and sunlight are the essential requirements for photosynthesis.  Complex chemical substances such as carbohydrates are the products of photosynthesis.  Solar energy is absorbed by the chlorophylls present in leaves/plants.  Oxygen is produced during photosynthesis.  Oxygen released in photosynthesis is utilised by living organisms for their survival.  Many fungi derive nutrition from dead and decaying matter.

The text explains that in this chapter you learnt that most of the plants are autotrophs.

The text explains that give a brief description of the process of synthesis of food in green plants.

The text explains that show with the help of a sketch that plants are the ultimate source of food.

The text explains that extended Learning — Activities and Projects 1.

The text explains that take two strips of black paper and cut out a small square in the centres.

The text explains that cover a part of two leaves with these papers and secure them with paper clips (Fig.

The text explains that observe the difference in the colour of the covered and the uncovered portions on the leaf.

The text explains that remove the strip and expose the covered part to the sunlight for 2–3 days and do the iodine test again.

The text explains that visit a green house if there is one near your place.

The text explains that find out how they regulate the amount of light, water and carbon dioxide to grow the plants.

The text explains that you can read more on the following website: www.phschool.com/science/biology_place/biocoach/photosynth/ overview.htm Fig.

The text explains that 1.9 Experiment to test the occurrence of photosynthesis Did you know?

The text explains that light is so important to plants that their leaves grow in many patterns so as to absorb maximum sunlight.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Science, Chapter 1: Nutrition in Plants

Thus, humans and animals are directly or indirectly dependent on plants. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch1 pg 1)

The nutrients enable living organisms to build their bodies, to grow, to repair damaged parts of their bodies and provide the energy to carry out life... (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch1 pg 1)
Nutrition is the mode of taking food by an organism and its utilisation by the body. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch1 pg 1)
The mode of nutrition in which organisms make food themselves from simple substances is called autotrophic (auto = self; trophos = nourishment) nutrition. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch1 pg 1)
Animals and most other organisms take in food prepared by plants. (NCERT Class 7 Science Ch1 pg 1)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1.1 M ODE OF NUTRITION IN PLANTS and 1.2 P HOTOSYNTHESIS — F OOD as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Nutrition in Plants; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Nutrition in Plants through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1.1 M ODE OF NUTRITION IN PLANTS and 1.2 P HOTOSYNTHESIS — F OOD to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
